tweets.text:
Polymarket on ceasefire/agreement between US and Iran. 

Rules:

A qualifying announcement requires clear public confirmation from the U.S. government that the U.S. has either:

1. Extended its commitment to the ceasefire, either as a dated/time-based extension (e.g. a 60 day extension) or through an explicit statement that the ceasefire has been extended.

2. Renewed the existing ceasefire as part of a broader peace agreement, ceasefire framework, or diplomatic agreement under which the ceasefire will continue.

tweets.text:
#Bitcoin / $BTC

This question challenged me to think critically about what else is a "bear case".

Outside of what I listed, the biggest one left on the table is cyclical performance.

The last 3 bull market peaks occurred ~536 days post their respective BTC halvings. All within 13 days of that number.

On the same note, the last 2 bear market bottoms occurred ~908 days post halving, both numbers within a few weeks of that number.

Which brings us to where we are now, ~769 days post halving... it's a small data set to go off of, and there are plenty of other external factors that affect each cycle differently - but I still would see it as a decent counter-argument to any *short term* bullish bias I have. We're still ~4.5 months out from what would be the bottom if we were to continue to follow what's been a consistent trend (e.g. bottom in October 2026).

Fwiw - if you look at the four month average price of BTC on either side of those bottoms, they were all incredible opportunities to stack more Bitcoin.

So like I also said in the interview - now isn't when you want to sit back and trade leverage because you're bored.

Instead, leverage your boredom and spend that time to build something great.

I'd love to see a bounce at $70K like I've shared over the last several weeks - but I'm fully prepared to use any extra time the market gives me to keep working harder and stack more.

Be more bullish.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Kaleo's bear case, in order of what he's actually worried about:

1. Equities top. Everything else follows from this. Bitcoin is weak enough that it can't absorb a macro shock independently.

2. Trump. Not a specific prediction. Just the acknowledgment that the distribution of https://t.co/pA5qkkehZg
tweets.full_text:
#Bitcoin / $BTC

This question challenged me to think critically about what else is a "bear case".

Outside of what I listed, the biggest one left on the table is cyclical performance.

The last 3 bull market peaks occurred ~536 days post their respective BTC halvings. All within 13 days of that number.

On the same note, the last 2 bear market bottoms occurred ~908 days post halving, both numbers within a few weeks of that number.

Which brings us to where we are now, ~769 days post halving... it's a small data set to go off of, and there are plenty of other external factors that affect each cycle differently - but I still would see it as a decent counter-argument to any *short term* bullish bias I have. We're still ~4.5 months out from what would be the bottom if we were to continue to follow what's been a consistent trend (e.g. bottom in October 2026).

Fwiw - if you look at the four month average price of BTC on either side of those bottoms, they were all incredible opportunities to stack more Bitcoin.

So like I also said in the interview - now isn't when you want to sit back and trade leverage because you're bored.

Instead, leverage your boredom and spend that time to build something great.

I'd love to see a bounce at $70K like I've shared over the last several weeks - but I'm fully prepared to use any extra time the market gives me to keep working harder and stack more.

Be more bullish.

tweets.text: $MSTR did not really buy back convertible bonds at a bargain. Strategy prepaid a zero-interest loan two years early at roughly present value. The so-called 8% discount reflects the time value of money. @Saylor reduced debt, but gave up free financing and burned badly needed cash.

Bloom Energy supplies Nebius, but who supplies Bloom?

$BE is the largest consumer of scandium on the planet, it's needed for their SOFC tech.

90% of scandium supply comes from China, and it's on the export control list.  

Sunrise Energy Resources (https://t.co/uMHxQtiW1j) is building the largest Western-controlled pure scandium mine on the planet.

I'm long.

tweets.text:
I was watching a video on Mark Laita's channel (Soft White Underbelly) a couple years ago and that woman was explaining how she overcome her drug addiction by becoming a gambling addict and later a sex addict instead

addiction is the most powerful motivation for the human brain: gambling addicts will walk their path to ruin despite knowing how it ends, addict parents will prioritize their fix over taking care of their children, etc

businesses have understood this extremely well and sell all kinds of addictions: cigarettes, booze, collecting things, doomscrolling...

so there are a lot of cheap addictions out there and that's why people end up addicted to looking at their phone or watching Netflix for example

on the other hand there are addictions that are actually net positives like working out, running, or some artistic endeavors

my point is if we're wired to becoming addicted to something, it's very important to be intentional about what that thing is
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: quitting porn was the easiest thing ever. you just have to replace it with an even more potent addiction. like cocaine
